peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.
High openness and low agreeableness drive a cautious but clear support for transparency, tempered by moderate trust in institutions and a libertarian skepticism of state control.

Very low openness and high conscientiousness plus a dangerous worldview motivate cautious support for transparency as a system-stabilizing reform, but low agreeableness and strong anti-egalitarianism keep enthusiasm from being strong.

High openness and low conscientiousness, combined with strong anti-elitism and a desire for system disruption, drive moderate support for algorithmic transparency as a check on opaque corporate power.

High openness and very high agreeableness drive support for transparency to protect users, but strong system justification and moderate trust in institutions temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.

High openness and agreeableness, combined with strong fairness/cheating concerns (low) and high care/harm, lead to moderate support for transparency as a protective measure, but libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) tempers it.

High openness and very low agreeableness, combined with strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation and deep distrust of institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on power, but skepticism that any mandate would be implemented fairly tempers enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong authoritarian aggression against rule-breaking and high system justification favor clear labeling of inauthentic actors, though low digital literacy limits full endorsement.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.
Strong fairness and anti-egalitarianism values, combined with high extraversion and a history of online harassment, produce firm support for disclosure to protect authentic discourse.

High dutifulness and system justification combine with strong competitive jungle worldview to back clear rules against deceptive actors, while low libertarianism and moderate civic nationalism temper any maximalism.

Strong authoritarian law-and-order preference and high threat sensitivity make this person clearly favor disclosure to police inauthentic behavior, though low trust in institutions tempers maximal enthusiasm.

Very high agreeableness and high moral concern for fairness (equality 89%) strongly favor disclosure to prevent deception, though low populism and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.

High care/harm (87.29) and moderate social dominance orientation (73.96) create a cautious endorsement of disclosure to protect vulnerable users, though low libertarian privacy (20.54) reduces full enthusiasm.

Very high sociocultural liberalism and populist anti-elitism drive a desire to expose manipulation by powerful actors, yet low trust in government and a dismissive-avoidant style limit endorsement to mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high agreeableness and tender-mindedness drive support for educational interventions, while high dutifulness and conventionalism align with promoting responsible citizenship.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and moderate openness (57.59) combined with strong care/harm moral foundation (93.03) and a competitive worldview (87.64) produce strong support for equipping citizens to navigate information, seen as a protective measure.
Very high openness and advanced digital literacy, paired with a strong sense of duty and community engagement, drive robust support for educational interventions.

Very low openness and a tradition-oriented socio-cultural tilt make educational interventions seem naive and likely to challenge established values, despite high conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.
High openness, strong altruism, and a philanthropic HEXAD profile drive clear support for media literacy as a prosocial, empowering solution, despite low digital literacy and intermittent access.

Very high openness and high conscientiousness align with educational solutions, and high internal political efficacy (80%) supports empowering citizens, but low trust in science (21%) slightly reduces enthusiasm.
High openness (69.03) and strong altruism (92.88) drive strong support for educational interventions, while low authoritarian submission (19.56) allows endorsement without fear of indoctrination.

High openness, advanced digital literacy, and strong value on personal growth align with empowering individuals, while deep distrust of institutions makes a top-down education program less appealing but still clearly supported as a bottom-up solution.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely high authoritarian submission and trust in government institutions strongly favor a dedicated agency, reinforced by high external political efficacy.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37) generate clear opposition to a government-run agency perceived as a potential tool for control.
Despite high trust in institutions, low agreeableness and a strong anti-domination moral foundation create mild opposition to a centralized agency perceived as potentially overreaching.

High authoritarian submission, conventionalism, and system justification favor official anti-disinformation bodies, but low openness and high collective narcissism breed suspicion of institutional motives, yielding only mild support.
Very low trust in government and institutions, combined with high anti-elitism and a competitive-jungle worldview, produce mild opposition to a centralized agency perceived as elite-controlled.
Low trust in government (23%) and moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation (42%) create skepticism toward a centralized agency, despite high agreeableness and concern for harm avoidance.

Low libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) and high system justification (85.5) create mild opposition to a centralized agency, as it conflicts with distrust of state control (10.55) and preference for organic solutions.

Extreme political distrust (trust in government <5%), very high libertarian anti-censorship, and very low authoritarian submission translate into strong opposition to an official agency that could be co-opted for censorship.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High ethnic nationalism and collective narcissism, combined with authoritarian punitive attitudes, support restricting foreign media influence.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (92.61), national superiority beliefs (79.72), and a competitive jungle worldview (87.64) combined with very low openness to cultural diversity (socio-cultural liberal-conservative mean 35.3) drive strong support for restricting foreign media influence.
Very high openness, cosmopolitan orientation, and dual citizenship status produce strong opposition to restricting foreign media, seen as a threat to cultural exchange.
Strong ethnic nationalism, restricted cosmopolitan orientation, high dangerous worldview, and very low anti-domination create firm endorsement of restricting foreign media as a protective measure.
Extremely low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, paired with high cultural openness, lead to clear opposition to restricting foreign media as xenophobic and illiberal.

Low nationalism (civic nationalism 12%) and moderate cosmopolitan orientation (37%) combined with high openness reduce support for restricting foreign media, while moderate TAN nationalism (60%) provides slight counterweight.
High cosmopolitan orientation (26.62) and low ethnic nationalism (36.61) produce clear opposition to restricting foreign media, seeing it as protectionist and counter to global information flow.

Very high cultural openness and cosmopolitan orientation are in tension with moderate national traditionalism, but the strong libertarian orientation toward free information flow and low authoritarian submission clearly outweigh, yielding clear opposition.

High openness and a value for integrity support researcher access, but low agreeableness and moderate privacy concerns keep support moderate.
Low openness, very high collective narcissism, and anti-egalitarianism drive skepticism of handing data to researchers perceived as out-group elites, despite moderate trust in expertise.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism strongly oppose freedom from moderation, favoring strict content controls.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37), strong free spirit user type (67.34), and very low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with distrust of institutions produce clear support for minimal moderation, valuing individual autonomy over platform control.
Strong anti-censorship orientation and a free-spirit user type, combined with low agreeableness, produce clear preference for minimal moderation despite some concern about harmful content.

Moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation and low agreeableness push for less moderation, but authoritarian submission and strong punitive attitudes rein in full support, producing mild preference.
A mix of high authoritarianism (favoring order) and low libertarianism (anti-censorship) yields mild support for less moderation, driven by distrust of elite-controlled platforms.

Moderate libertarian anti-censorship (42%) and high openness support freedom from moderation, but very high agreeableness and concern for harm avoidance (59%) create ambivalence about unmoderated spaces.

Low libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) and high agreeableness (77.08) lead to mild opposition to complete freedom from moderation, as it could enable harm, though high openness values (70.06) create some ambivalence.

Extremely high libertarian anti-censorship (87.94), very low authoritarian submission, and very low trust in all institutions combine with very high openness to produce near-maximal support for minimal content moderation.

High dutifulness and system justification back protections as procedural fairness, but low openness, competitive worldview, and collective narcissism dampen enthusiasm, resulting in mild support.
